summary: My name is Grace Deaneh, and I was born on March 23, 1994, in Monrovia, Liberia. When I was 11 years old, my mom moved to the United States for a better life and left me with my uncle in Liberia. After eight years, in 2013, my mom came back to Liberia to bring me to the U.S., and she gave me a colorful jacket that has been very special to me ever since. That jacket kept me warm during my first winter in Minnesota and remains a cherished memory.

I was born in Monrovia, the capital city of Liberia, in 1994. Monrovia is a warm and beautiful place, and it was where I lived with my uncle after my mom moved to the United States. It was also the city where my mom returned in 2013 to bring me to America. Monrovia holds many memories for me, especially from my childhood before I moved abroad.

Liberia is my home country, and it is very warm with a rainy season from November to April and a dry season from May to October. I lived there with my uncle after my mom left for the United States following the war in Liberia. In 2013, my mom came back to Liberia to bring me to the U.S., marking a big change in my life. I still remember the weather and seasons there, which are very different from the cold winters I experienced later.

My mom moved to the United States after the war in Liberia to seek a better life, but she couldn't bring me with her at first. In 2013, I finally joined her in the U.S., traveling from Liberia through Chicago and then to Minnesota. The U.S. was very different from Liberia, especially with the cold weather and snow I saw for the first time in Chicago. Living here has been a big adjustment, but I am grateful for the opportunities it has given me.

When my mom and I arrived in Chicago in January 2013, it was my first time seeing snow and experiencing cold weather. Coming from Liberia, where it is warm most of the year, Chicago felt very different and surprising to me. We only stayed briefly before moving on to Minnesota, but that moment in Chicago marked the start of my new life in the United States.

After arriving in Chicago, my mom and I traveled to Minnesota, where the weather was even colder, around 20 degrees. I wore the jacket my mom gave me to keep warm, and it helped me through my first winter here without getting sick. Minnesota has been my home since then, and I continue to rely on that jacket every winter as a reminder of my journey and my mom's love.
